title: Creation / Adam
similarities: Humanity is specially created by God.
The first humans dwell in a garden-like setting.
Disobedience leads to exile from ease.
Divine guidance continues after the fall.

differences.description: Christian readings often connect Adam to doctrines of sin and redemption through Christ.
Qur’anic framing stresses repentance and guidance without inherited guilt.

commentary_notes: Creation narratives ground each tradition’s view of human purpose.
A timeline view can later link Adam traditions with prophetic succession.

citations.note: Reference anchors only; verse text intentionally left as placeholder.
Reference anchors only; verse text intentionally left as placeholder.
Reference anchors only; translation source still under review for licensing/usage consistency.

alignment_rows.note: All three traditions open Adam’s story with divine initiative in creating or inaugurating the created order.

torah.notes: Classical opening of Torah/Tanakh creation account.

christian_bible.notes: Shared canonical opening with Hebrew Bible text tradition.

quran.notes: Begins Adam cycle with divine declaration to the angels.

alignment_rows.note: Torah/Tanakh and Christian Bible narrate ordered creation sequence; Qur’an references divine ordering across passages.
All traditions mark humanity as a distinct act of creation with elevated role.

christian_bible.notes: Shared Genesis wording in Christian canon.

quran.notes: Adam formed and enlivened by God.

alignment_rows.note: Purpose language appears as dominion/stewardship in Genesis and khalifah framing in Qur’an.

torah.notes: Mandate to fill and tend creation.

christian_bible.notes: Often read as vocation and stewardship.

alignment_rows.note: Eden/Jannah setting provides initial state of provision and nearness.

torah.notes: Garden in Eden and charge to keep it.

quran.notes: Adam and spouse placed in the garden.

alignment_rows.note: Each tradition includes a divine command tied to a specific forbidden tree/action.
Transgression follows deceptive prompting; narrative details and theological emphases differ.

torah.notes: Serpent episode and eating the fruit.

christian_bible.notes: Foundational fall narrative in Christian theology.

alignment_rows.note: Departure from the garden marks a shift to earthly toil and moral testing.
Mercy themes are present in all three, though explicit repentance language is strongest in Qur’anic rendering.

torah.notes: God clothes the humans after judgment.

christian_bible.notes: Read as sign of continuing divine care.

quran.notes: Adam receives words and is forgiven.

alignment_rows.note: Post-fall life includes continued divine instruction; Torah/Christian text here is broader-canonical rather than a single Adam-only command.

torah.notes: Judgment sayings frame human condition and future path.

christian_bible.notes: Often tied to later salvation-history readings.

quran.notes: Guidance promised for earthly life after descent.
